description: Core set registry for python PQC API reference

python_image_build_schema.compatibility_semantics: compatibility values are minimum Python requirements unless a closed range is explicitly provided

discovery_service.name: PQC Discovery Service
discovery_service.description: Master discovery service listing all available API endpoints

languages.name: Python
languages._comment_multi_stage: Dockerfiles generated by lang_python.py's make_dockerfile() are multi-stage (2026-07-11): a `builder` stage carries the full build-essential/gcc/libffi-dev/libssl-dev toolchain (plus cmake/ninja/git for liboqs-python) and does `pip install --user`; the final stage starts fresh from the SAME base image and only `COPY --from=builder /root/.local /root/.local` (Python's own `site` module auto-discovers `~/.local/lib/python{X.Y}/site-packages` for a matching interpreter version, no PYTHONPATH needed). Confirmed via a real docker build+run that this shrinks a typical image by ~70-73% (e.g. cryptography 45.0.1: 485MB -> 137MB; liboqs-python 0.14: 611MB -> 166MB; PyNaCl 1.5.0: 476MB -> 129MB; M2Crypto 0.42: 435MB -> 131MB) with zero functional change -- every combo was previously carrying a full C compiler toolchain that only a minority of libraries (M2Crypto, liboqs-python, and old pre-abi3 wheel versions needing a source build) ever actually used. Runtime needs NO extra apt-get for OpenSSL/libffi -- confirmed both `python:{ver}-slim` (bookworm: libssl.so.3/libffi.so.8) and `-slim-bullseye` (libssl.so.1.1/libffi.so.7) already ship them as a dependency of Python's own ssl/_ctypes modules. The one exception is PyNaCl, whose `libsodium` is NOT part of any base image -- its final stage gets an explicit `libsodium23` (covers the rare case pip falls back to a source build instead of a prebuilt wheel).

frameworks.name: Flask
frameworks.notes: Compatibility is the minimum Python requirement for the major line; later minor releases in the same major may require newer Python versions. Majors 0/1 genuinely break on Python 3.14 -- confirmed via a real docker build+run using this project's own FW_VERSION_EXTRA-pinned dependency set (Jinja2<3.1.0/MarkupSafe<2.1.0/itsdangerous, etc, see lang_python.py): `flask/helpers.py`'s `get_root_path()`/`_find_package_path()` call `pkgutil.get_loader()`, deprecated since Python 3.12 and actually REMOVED in 3.14, raising `AttributeError: module 'pkgutil' has no attribute 'get_loader'` at `Flask(__name__)` construction time (not at import time). Verified the exact boundary directly: works on 3.11/3.12/3.13, fails only on 3.14. Majors 2/3 don't share this ceiling -- both rewrote this code path onto `importlib` and are confirmed working on 3.14 directly.

frameworks.name: Django
frameworks.notes: Compatibility is the minimum Python requirement for the major line; later minor releases in the same major may require newer Python versions. Bucket '0' is Django's real pre-1.0 line (0.90-0.96, 2005-11-16 to 2007-03-23, confirmed via PyPI/release-note dates) -- the last 'unstable' releases before the 1.0 stability baseline; recorded as an explicit reference row (compatibility: []) rather than silently omitted, per this project's standing convention for historical versions. Not independently build-verified this pass (ancient Python 2.3/2.4-era code, no PyPI-installable artifact for most of this range -- it predates PyPI's own package-upload conventions).

frameworks.name: FastAPI
frameworks.notes: FastAPI currently uses the 0.x major line. Compatibility is minimum by major line; newer 0.x minors can raise the minimum Python requirement.

frameworks.name: Tornado
frameworks.notes: Confirmed working end-to-end via a real docker build+run on Python 3.12 (major 6, `tornado.web.Application` + `IOLoop.current().start()`). 0.x (2010-05-18) is a single ancient release, not independently version-bucketed (pre-PyPI-metadata era). Majors 1/2 (2010-2014) genuinely fail to even import on ANY Python 3.x -- confirmed via a real docker build: `tornado/web.py` uses Python-2-only tuple-parameter-unpacking lambda syntax (`lambda (l, s): ...`), a hard SyntaxError on Python 3 regardless of version. Majors 4/5 genuinely break starting Python 3.10 -- confirmed via a real docker build: `tornado/httputil.py` subclasses `collections.MutableMapping`, an alias Python removed from the `collections` module itself (moved to `collections.abc`) in 3.10; verified the exact boundary (works on 3.9, AttributeError on 3.10) for both majors directly, not assumed identical to major 6's floor. Only major 6 fixed this properly and has no upper bound.

frameworks.name: aiohttp
frameworks.notes: Confirmed working end-to-end via a real docker build+run on Python 3.12 (major 3, `web.Application()` + `web.run_app()`). 0.x (2013-2016) predates asyncio's own stabilization in the stdlib and is not independently version-bucketed in this pass. A 4.0.0a1 alpha exists on PyPI but no stable 4.0 has shipped as of this writing -- not tracked. Majors 1/2's own PyPI classifiers claim 3.4-3.6 support, but 1.x genuinely fails to even import outside Python 3.6 exactly -- confirmed via a real docker build: `helpers.py` uses `asyncio.async` (an attribute name that became a SyntaxError once `async` was reserved as a keyword in Python 3.7), and separately fails on 3.5 too (verified) -- 3.6 is the only real working floor, not a range. Both majors 1 and 2 ALSO leave `async-timeout` (a transitive dependency) completely unversioned in their own setup.py -- pip resolves it to async-timeout's latest release (4.0.2), which itself needs a newer Python than these majors target, causing a real `TypeError: function() argument 1 must be code, not str` at import time (a stale/incompatible-bytecode symptom of the version mismatch) -- fixed with an explicit pin to async-timeout 3.0.1 (`FW_VERSION_EXTRA` in lang_python.py), the same class of unpinned-transitive-dependency bug already documented for Go's Iris/Echo and Node's several frameworks in this project.

frameworks.name: CherryPy
frameworks.notes: Confirmed working end-to-end via a real docker build+run on Python 3.12 (major 18, `cherrypy.quickstart()` + `@cherrypy.expose`/`@cherrypy.tools.json_out()`). CherryPy released 16 numbered majors between 2015 and 2018 (3 through 18) -- confirmed via PyPI's real release history -- reflecting loose semver discipline in that era rather than 16 genuinely distinct API eras, the same pattern already documented for Node's Hapi package. Majors 4-16 are real, PyPI-installable releases but are NOT individually version-bucketed here (a disclosed density tradeoff, not a claim that they don't work); 3 and 17/18 were chosen as the long-lived-stable and current/final eras respectively. 18.x is CherryPy's last major (still receiving patches). Major 3 genuinely breaks starting Python 3.8 -- confirmed via a real docker build: `_cperror.py` does `from cgi import escape`, removed from the `cgi` module in Python 3.8 (deprecated since 3.2); verified the exact boundary directly (works on 3.7, ImportError on 3.8). Major 17 genuinely breaks starting Python 3.11, unlike 18 -- confirmed via a real docker build: `_cpdispatch.py` does `getargspec = inspect.getargspec`, removed from `inspect` in 3.11 (deprecated since 3.0); this is unrelated to the FW_PIP mechanism's own version-pin resolution since frameworks use a pip range (`>=17.0,<18.0`) rather than this file's per-version PyPI lookup, so pip always installs 17's actual latest patch, which still has this call. 18.x's own maintenance patches fixed it (confirmed working on 3.11 and 3.12 directly) -- don't assume adjacent CherryPy majors 17/18 share a Python ceiling just because they were released two months apart.

frameworks.name: Bottle
frameworks.notes: Bottle has never left the 0.x line in its entire history (0.4.3 through the current 0.13.x) -- confirmed via PyPI's full release list, the same single-major situation as this registry's own FastAPI entry. Confirmed working end-to-end via a real docker build+run on Python 3.12 (`@route('/')` + `run()`, single-file micro-framework by design).

frameworks.name: Pyramid
frameworks.notes: Confirmed working end-to-end via a real docker build+run on Python 3.12 (major 2, `Configurator()` + `add_route`/`add_view`, plain WSGI via `wsgiref.simple_server`). One real, found-the-hard-way fix: `pyramid.response.Response(text, content_type=...)` requires an explicit `charset` kwarg when the body is a text (not bytes) value -- omitting it throws `TypeError: You cannot set the body to a text value without a charset` at request time (a real docker-run failure, not a build-time error), confirmed by adding `charset="UTF-8"`. `pcreate`/scaffolding is optional, not required, for this pattern -- no CLI scaffolding was needed.

cryptography_libs.name: PyNaCl
cryptography_libs.notes: A Python binding to the Networking and Cryptography library (NaCl), which provides high-level cryptographic functions. It is designed to be easy to use and secure, with support for modern cryptographic primitives. The library is actively maintained and widely used in the Python community.

cryptography_libs.name: cryptography
cryptography_libs.notes: Widely used library with support for modern cryptographic primitives, including post-quantum algorithms. Actively maintained and regularly updated with security patches.

cryptography_libs.name: PyCryptodome
cryptography_libs.notes: A self-contained Python package of low-level cryptographic primitives. It is a fork of the now unmaintained PyCrypto library and includes support for various cryptographic algorithms, including some post-quantum schemes.

cryptography_libs.name: M2Crypto
cryptography_libs.notes: A Python wrapper for OpenSSL. While it provides access to OpenSSL's cryptographic functions, its support for modern and post-quantum algorithms is limited. The library has seen less active development in recent years, which may impact its suitability for new projects.

cryptography_libs.name: PyCrypto
cryptography_libs.notes: An older cryptographic library that is no longer maintained. It has known security vulnerabilities and lacks support for modern cryptographic algorithms, including post-quantum schemes. It is not recommended for use in new projects.

cryptography_libs.name: liboqs-python
cryptography_libs.notes: The official Open Quantum Safe project's Python binding (github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs-python), a ctypes wrapper around the liboqs C library -- the Python equivalent of Go's liboqs-go / PHP's php-liboqs / .NET's LibOQS.NET / Node's liboqs-node, and the one language in this project that previously had NO liboqs binding tracked at all despite every other language having one. Requires the liboqs C library built from source in the same Dockerfile stage (git clone + cmake + ninja, the same recipe already used for the other languages' liboqs bindings) plus `LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/local/lib` so the ctypes loader can find the resulting shared library at runtime -- no compilation of the Python package itself (it's pure ctypes, not a C extension). Confirmed working end-to-end via a real docker build+run: `import oqs; oqs.KeyEncapsulation('ML-KEM-768').generate_keypair()` returns a real 1184-byte ML-KEM-768 public key -- unlike Node's liboqs-node, this binding is built against a current liboqs release and exposes the final NIST algorithm names directly, no draft-name caveat needed. '0.15's compatibility floor was originally (wrongly) recorded as '3.9+', the same as '0.14' -- a real `pip install` failure on Python 3.9 surfaced PyPI's own per-file `requires_python` metadata directly: 0.14.1 declares `>=3.9`, but 0.15.0 raised it to `>=3.10` (same bug class as this file's Authlib entry above). Corrected to '3.10+'; Python 3.9 now only gets the 0.14 bucket for this library.

cryptography_libs.name: pyOpenSSL
cryptography_libs.notes: Confirmed working end-to-end via a real docker build+run on Python 3.12 (major 26, `import OpenSSL`). Switched to calendar-year versioning at major 16 (2016) -- every year from 16 through 26 has at least one real release (confirmed via PyPI's full history); this registry tracks a representative subset (0 legacy, 17/22/26 spanning the calendar-versioning era) rather than all 11 yearly majors, a density tradeoff disclosed here rather than silently applied. '0.15.1's own release_date was ALSO originally wrong here (recorded as 2008-06-04; PyPI's real upload_time is 2015-04-14) -- corrected. '0.15.1' is marked `available: false`: a real docker build+run investigation (not a bare-import check) found it hard-broken on every Python version this registry tracks, for a GENUINE, exhaustively-verified reason, not a convenience exclusion. Its `rand.py`/`SSL.py`/`crypto.py` call OpenSSL-1.0.x-only symbols (`ERR_load_RAND_strings`, `SSL_ST_INIT` -- part of OpenSSL's pre-1.1.0 explicit SSL state machine, replaced by an enum in 1.1.0 -- and `GEN_EMAIL`) via `cryptography`'s cffi `_lib` binding, which it depends on completely unversioned (`cryptography (>=0.7)`, no upper bound, confirmed via PyPI's own requires_dist). Bisected exhaustively: `cryptography` 3.4.8 (the LAST release before its 2021 renumbering jump straight to 35.0.0 -- no intermediate real version exists) still lacks `SSL_ST_INIT`; genuinely ancient cryptography releases contemporaneous with pyOpenSSL 0.15.1's 2015 vintage (1.7.2, 1.9) have no installable wheel for any currently-tracked Python and fail to build from source against EITHER this project's default Bookworm base (OpenSSL 3.x headers) OR a Bullseye base (OpenSSL 1.1.1 headers) -- `SSL_ST_INIT` requires genuine OpenSSL-1.0.x C headers that neither base image ships, and no practical Docker base for currently-tracked Python versions does either. pyOpenSSL 17.5.0 (2017) does NOT share this ceiling -- it never calls `ERR_load_RAND_strings`/`SSL_ST_INIT`, only hits a DIFFERENT now-removed symbol (`GEN_EMAIL`, in `crypto.py`), fixed by the `cryptography<36` pin in `LIB_VERSION_EXTRA` (see `lang_python.py`) -- don't assume adjacent old pyOpenSSL majors share either the same broken symbol or the same fix.

cryptography_libs.name: ecdsa
cryptography_libs.notes: Pure-Python ECDSA implementation, has never left the 0.x line in its entire history (0.6 through the current 0.19.x) -- confirmed via PyPI's full release list, the same single-major situation as this registry's Bottle/ecdsa/FastAPI entries. Confirmed working end-to-end via a real docker build+run on Python 3.12.

cryptography_libs.name: Authlib
cryptography_libs.notes: OAuth/OIDC/JOSE (JWT/JWS/JWE/JWK) implementation. Confirmed working end-to-end via a real docker build+run on Python 3.12 (major 1, `import authlib`). No ML-DSA/PQC JOSE support as of this writing (same gap already noted for Java's Nimbus JOSE+JWT in this project). '1.7.2's compatibility floor was originally (wrongly) recorded as '3.6+' -- a real `pip install` failure on Python 3.9 surfaced PyPI's own classifier data: 1.7.0/1.7.1/1.7.2 all declare `Requires-Python >=3.10` (a jump made partway through the 1.x line, not from 1.0.0). Corrected to '3.10+'; Python 3.6-3.9 fall back to only the 0.15.6 bucket for this library, same as pre-1.7 Authlib users would see in real life.
